---
title: "Building stock-and-flow models"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
bibliography: references/references.bib
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Building stock-and-flow models}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 6, # Set default figure width to 6 inches
  fig.height = 4, # Set default figure height to 4 inches
  fig.align = "center", # Center all figures
  out.width = "80%" # Display figures at 80% of container width
  # fig.retina = 2,         # Optimize for retina displays
  # dpi = 96               # Standard DPI for web
)
```

```{r, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
library(sdbuildR)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
# Disable WebGL: many plotly widgets per HTML page can exceed the browser WebGL
# context limit and render blank. SVG always renders.
options(sdbuildR.webgl = FALSE)
```

```{r libraries, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```


```{r include=FALSE}
# Set this to TRUE to recreate the figures in the paper
recreate_figs <- interactive()
```

```{r include=FALSE}
# Helper function
sprintf_arg <- function(template, values) {
  for (nm in names(values)) {
    template <- gsub(paste0("%\\(", nm, "\\)s"), values[[nm]], template)
  }
  return(template)
}
font_family <- "Times New Roman"

# Plotting parameters
filepath_base <- this.path::this.dir()
filepath_figs <- file.path(filepath_base, "figs_tutorial2026")

if (recreate_figs) {
  dir.create(filepath_figs, showWarnings = FALSE)
}

VARS <- c("engagement", "performance", "energy", "demands", "resources", "motivation_rate")
PALETTE <- c("Geyser", "Temps", "Spectral", "Zissou 1", "Roma")[5]
COLORS <- grDevices::hcl.colors(n = 50, palette = PALETTE)
# scales::show_col(COLORS)

inflow_col <- adjustcolor(COLORS[10], alpha.f = 1)
outflow_col <- adjustcolor(COLORS[3], alpha.f = 1)

inflow_fill <- adjustcolor(inflow_col, alpha.f = .5)
outflow_fill <- adjustcolor(outflow_col, alpha.f = .5)

# scales::show_col( grDevices::hcl.colors(n = 50, palette = "Greens"))
increase_col <- adjustcolor(
  grDevices::hcl.colors(n = 50, palette = "Greens")[20],
  alpha.f = 1)
decrease_col <- adjustcolor(COLORS[2], alpha.f = 1)
# scales::show_col(c(increase_col, decrease_col))
increase_fill <- adjustcolor(increase_col, alpha.f = .8)
decrease_fill <- adjustcolor(decrease_col, alpha.f = .4)

COLORS_alpha <- adjustcolor(COLORS, alpha.f = .25)

COLORS <- COLORS[c(37, 9, 32, 1, 47, 17)]
stock_col <- adjustcolor(COLORS[1], alpha.f = .75)
flow_col <- COLORS[6]

health_path <- adjustcolor(COLORS[4], alpha.f = .75)
motivation_path <- adjustcolor(COLORS[1], alpha.f = .85)
health_path_fill <- adjustcolor(COLORS[4], alpha.f = .25)
motivation_path_fill <- adjustcolor(COLORS[1], alpha.f = .25)
performance_path <- adjustcolor(COLORS[2], alpha.f = .75)
performance_path_fill <- adjustcolor(COLORS[2], alpha.f = .25)

line_width <- 1.5
line_width_stock <- line_width + 2

font_size <- 16
font_size_xlab <- round(font_size * 1.4)
font_size_legend <- round(font_size * 1.1)
font_size_subplot <- font_size # round(font_size * 1.5)
xlab <- "Time (days)"

# Finer-grained simulation
save_at_fine <- .1 # .01
save_at_coarse <- 1

# Annotation template
annot <- list(xanchor = "center", yanchor = "middle", showarrow = FALSE, xref = "paper", yref = "paper", font = list(size = font_size_subplot, family = font_family))
annot_font <- list(family = font_family, size = font_size)
annot_legend <- list(
  orientation = "h", # orientation
  x = 0.5, # Center horizontally
  y = -0.15, # Below the plot
  xanchor = "center",
  yanchor = "top",
  # traceorder = "normal",
  # tracegroupgap = 0,
  # itemwidth = 15,
  font = list(size = font_size_legend)
)
annot_xlab <- list(
  text = xlab,
  font = list(
    family = font_family,
    size = font_size_xlab
  ),
  xref = "paper",
  yref = "paper",
  xanchor = "center",
  yanchor = "top",
  x = 0.5, y = -.1,
  showarrow = FALSE
)

rm_grid <- function(pl) {
  plotly::layout(pl,
    xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, showline = TRUE),
    yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, showline = TRUE)
  )
}
```


Stock-and-flow models represent systems as states (stocks) that accumulate over time with processes (flows) that change these variables. In this vignette, we will demonstrate how to create stock-and-flow models from scratch using sdbuildR. It covers the basics of stock-and-flow modelling in the context of psychology with an example of burnout. Note that this vignette serves as online supplemental material A accompanying the paper *Formalizing Psychological Theory with sdbuildR: A Stock-and-Flow Modelling Tutorial in R* by Evers et al. (under review). To reproduce the figures in the paper, please see the corresponding .Rmd file.

## Stock-and-flow models

Stock-and-flow models conceptualize systems in terms of quantities that accumulate (i.e., stocks) and the processes (i.e., flows) that change them over time. Stocks are like the amount of water in a bathtub: they store the effects of past and present flows. Stocks must be able to increase and decrease, and should be measurable at a single moment in time. Inflows -- water from the tap -- raise the stock, while outflows -- water through the drain -- lower it. As such, flows represent the rates at which stocks change, measured in units per time (e.g., litre per minute). The net rate of change in the water level is determined by the difference between the inflows and outflows. In this way, a stock functions as a memory of past activity: it increases when inflows exceed outflows and decreases when outflows outpace inflows. Without an outflow, the water remains in the bathtub; without an inflow, the bathtub stays empty. This structure is the foundation of stock-and-flow models, where stocks represent the state of a system, and flows represent the processes that alter that state over time.


```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Time points for the four phases of stock change (rise and fall)
t0 <- 0   # start (inflow == outflow, stock flat)
t1 <- 2   # equal -> rise: inflow > outflow
t2 <- 4   
t3 <- 6   # rise  -> fall: outflow > inflow
t4 <- 8   


sfm <- stockflow() |>
stock(Stock, eqn = 1) |>
custom_func(func1, eqn = pulse(times, start = !!t1, width = !!t2 - !!t1, height = .5)) |>
custom_func(func2, eqn = pulse(times, start = !!t3, width = !!t4 - !!t3, height = .5)) |>
# custom_func(func3, eqn = pulse(times, start = 4, 
# # Make width large just in case
# width = 4, height = .5)) |>
flow(Inflow, eqn = 1 + func1(t), to = Stock) |>
flow(Outflow, eqn = 1 + func2(t), from = Stock) |>
sim_settings(only_stocks=FALSE)

sim <- simulate(sfm, stop = 10)
df_stock <- as.data.frame(sim, vars = "Stock")
df_in    <- as.data.frame(sim, vars = "Inflow")
df_out   <- as.data.frame(sim, vars = "Outflow")
df_change <- df_in
df_change$value <- df_change$value - df_out$value


size_marker <- 8
line_width_thick  <- 5

# Stock value at each time point (robust to non-exact time matches)
stock_at <- function(tt) df_stock$value[which.min(abs(df_stock$time - tt))]
y0 <- stock_at(t0); y1 <- stock_at(t1); y2 <- stock_at(t2); y3 <- stock_at(t3); y4 <- stock_at(t4)

# --- Top panel: stock with a change indicator per phase ----------------------
fig1 <- plotly::plot_ly(df_stock, x = ~time, y = ~value, type = 'scatter',
showlegend = FALSE,
                mode = 'lines', name = 'Stock', line = list(color = stock_col, width = line_width_thick)) 
                
                
# markers at the phase boundaries
fig1 <- fig1 |>
  plotly::add_trace(x = c(t1), y = c(y1), mode = 'markers', name = 't1', showlegend = FALSE,
            marker = list(color = increase_col, size = size_marker)) |>
  plotly::add_trace(x = c(t2), y = c(y2), mode = 'markers', name = 't2', showlegend = FALSE,
            marker = list(color = increase_col, size = size_marker)) |>
  plotly::add_trace(x = c(t3), y = c(y3), mode = 'markers', name = 't3', showlegend = FALSE,
            marker = list(color = decrease_col, size = size_marker)) |>
  plotly::add_trace(x = c(t4), y = c(y4), mode = 'markers', name = 't4', showlegend = FALSE,
            marker = list(color = decrease_col, size = size_marker))


# --- Bottom panel: inflow & outflow with the difference shaded ----------------
fig2 <- plotly::plot_ly() 

# Vertical reference lines at the end-of-phase levels (rise and fall phases)            
add_vert_lines <- function(fig, y_range = c(-2.5, 2.5)) {
  fig |> 
    plotly::add_trace(x = c(t1, t1), y = y_range, mode = 'lines', name = 't1',
             line = list(width = 2, dash = 'dot', color = increase_col), showlegend = FALSE) |>
    plotly::add_trace(x = c(t2, t2), y = y_range, mode = 'lines', name = 't2',
              line = list(width = 2, dash = 'dot', color = increase_col), showlegend = FALSE) |>
    plotly::add_trace(x = c(t3, t3), y = y_range, mode = 'lines', name = 't3',
              line = list(width = 2, dash = 'dot', color = decrease_col), showlegend = FALSE) |>
    plotly::add_trace(x = c(t4, t4), y = y_range, mode = 'lines', name = 't4',
              line = list(width = 2, dash = 'dot', color = decrease_col), showlegend = FALSE)
}

fig1 <- add_vert_lines(fig1)
fig2 <- add_vert_lines(fig2)

# Net flow
fig2 <- fig2 |>
  plotly::add_trace(data = df_change, x = ~time, y = ~value, type = 'scatter', mode = 'lines',
  showlegend = FALSE, fill = 'tozeroy',
  fillcolor = ifelse(df_change$value >= 0, increase_fill, decrease_fill),
            name = 'Net Flow', line = list(width = 0)) |>

  plotly::add_trace(data = df_change, x = ~time, y = ~value, type = 'scatter', mode = 'lines',
  showlegend = FALSE, 
            name = 'Net Flow', line = list(color = flow_col, width = line_width_thick)) 


# --- Combine panels ----------------------------------------------------------
sp_margin <- 0.12                       # vertical gap between the two panels (paper)
top_margin <- 80 #40
pad_axis <- .1
pad_axis2 <- .25
fig <- plotly::subplot(fig1, fig2, nrows = 2, shareX = FALSE, titleY = TRUE,
heights = c(.5, .5),
                       margin = sp_margin)

# --- Phase labels above each panel -------------------------------------------
annot_ <- utils::modifyList(annot, list(
  font = list(size = font_size_subplot - 2, family = font_family)
))
top_y    <- 1.0                         # top labels sit just above the top panel
bot_y    <- (0.5 - sp_margin) + 0.01    # bottom labels sit just above bottom panel
top_text_y <- c(bot_y + c(.265, .35, .58, .35, .265))
bot_text_y <- c(bot_y - c(.195, .065, .195, .39, .195))
top_angles <- c(0, -43, 0, 43, 0)
bot_angles <- c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
# bot_y    <- (0.5 - sp_margin) - 0.1

# phase mid-points on the (shared) data x-axis, used to centre each label
centers  <- c((t0 + t1)/2, (t1 + t2)/2, (t2 + t3)/2, (t3 + t4)/2, (t4 + 10)/2)
centers_top <- centers
centers_top[c(2, 4)] <- centers_top[c(2, 4)] + c(-.17, .17)   # shift the bottom labels for the rise/fall phases
top_text <- paste0("<i>", c("Static", "Increases", "Static", "Decreases", "Static"), "</i>")
bot_text <- paste0("<i>", c("Inflow ≡ Outflow", "Inflow > Outflow", "Inflow ≡ Outflow",
              "Inflow < Outflow", "Inflow ≡ Outflow"), "</i>")

phase_label <- function(text, x, y, angle = 0) list(
  x = x, y = y, text = text, xref = 'x', yref = 'paper',
  textangle = angle,
  xanchor = 'center', yanchor = 'bottom', showarrow = FALSE, font = annot_
)


label_anns <- c(
  lapply(seq_along(top_text), function(i) phase_label(top_text[i], centers_top[i], y = top_text_y[i], angle = top_angles[i])),
  lapply(seq_along(bot_text), function(i) phase_label(bot_text[i], centers[i], y = bot_text_y[i], angle = bot_angles[i]))
)

fig <- fig |> 
plotly::layout(
  # unname(): Map() returns a named list, which plotly mis-parses
  annotations = unname(c(
  label_anns)),
  margin = list(t = top_margin)                 # headroom for the top labels
) |>
  plotly::layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "", showline = TRUE, zerolinewidth = 0, showgrid = FALSE),
    xaxis2 = list(title = "", showline = TRUE, showgrid = FALSE,
                              zerolinecolor = '#ffff',
 zerolinewidth = 0),
    yaxis = list(title = "Units", showgrid = FALSE,
    # showline = TRUE,
     range = c(1 - pad_axis, 2 + pad_axis),
     tickvals = c(1, 2), ticktext = c("1", "2")),
    yaxis2 = list(title = "Units / Time", 
    zerolinewidth = 0, showgrid = FALSE,
    showline = TRUE, 
    range = c(-.5 - pad_axis2, .5 + pad_axis2), 
                  tickvals = c(-.5, .5), ticktext = c("-0.5", "0.5")),
    font = annot_font,
    annotations = list(
      utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(text = "<i>A. Stock-and-Flow Diagram</i>", x = 0.5, y = top_y + .22)),
      utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(text = "<i>B. Timeseries of Stock</i>", x = 0.5, y = top_y + .13)),
      utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(text = "<i>C. Timeseries of Change in Stock</i>", x = 0.5, 
      y = bot_y + .11)),
      phase_label("<i>Area = Increase\nin stock</i>", x = centers[2], 
      
      y = bot_y - .2
      ),
      phase_label("<i>Area = Decrease\nin stock</i>", x = centers[4], y = bot_y - .325),
      utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(text = "Time", x = 0.5, y = -0.06))
    )
  )


if (recreate_figs) {
  export_plot(fig, file.path(filepath_figs, "build_stock_flow_phases.pdf"),
    width = 6, height = 4.2
  )
}


fig
```


Stock-and-flow models provide an intuitive way to formalize psychological theories as many are fundamentally concerned with change over time. Despite the physical connotation of the term, stocks need not be tangible: emotions, knowledge, beliefs, perceptions, stress, motivation, and trust are all examples of psychological constructs that accumulate over time, often in response to experience or behaviour. The processes that drive these changes -- such as emotion regulation, coping, and learning -- are the flows, specifying what causes psychological states to increase or decrease.


Stock-and-flow models are easiest to understand through a worked example. We will create a simplified model of burnout. Before building it from scratch, we load it from the model library:

```{r}
sfm <- stockflow("burnout", version = 1)
print(sfm)
```

`sfm` is a stock-and-flow model object, containing a single stock representing energy, an inflow for recovery, and an outflow for energy depletion. 
In addition, the model contains two other variable types: constants and auxiliaries. Throughout the tutorial, we use the term "variable" for any part of the system, be that a stock, flow, constant, or auxiliary. Though this usage may differ from other scientific fields, we here choose to adhere to system dynamics terminology [@ford_system_2019; @Sterman2000BusinessWorld].
Constants are static parameters that do not change over the time course of the simulation. In contrast, auxiliaries are dynamic, meaning they are computed anew at each step. They are intermediate variables used in flow equations or to monitor other dynamic quantities. To illustrate the difference, a constant defined as `runif(1)` will be fixed to a random number at the beginning of the simulation, whereas an equivalently defined auxiliary will draw a new number each time step.
Lastly, the object contains simulation settings such as the total duration, the timestep (`dt`) specifying the temporal resolution of the simulation, and a solver (`euler`) indicating the numerical technique used to generate output from the model [for more details, see @deSolve]. All variables are saved in the simulation output, which can be reduced to saving only stocks or specific variables for computational efficiency.

Plotting the model shows its stock-and-flow diagram:

```{r}
plot(sfm, show_constants = TRUE)
```

To assess the model's dynamics, we simulate it over time and visualise the resulting timeseries:

```{r}
sfm |> simulate() |> plot()
```


Above, we use the pipe operator `|>` to pass the result of an expression to the next expression as its first argument. As shown above, energy increases over time but then stabilizes at a fixed level when the outflow of energy depletion meets the constant inflow of recovery. 

We now build this same model from scratch in iterative steps. The table below provides an overview of each model revision and the behaviour it produces. 


```{r echo = FALSE}
eqn_interpretation_table <- data.frame(
  Panel = LETTERS[1:5],
  `Recovery eqn (inflow)` = c("", "", "", "`0.3`", "`recovery_rate`"),
  `Depletion eqn (outflow)` = c("", "`0.05`", "`0.05 * energy`", "`0.05 * energy`", "`0.05 * energy`"),
  Interpretation = c(
    "No process of change",
    "Energy decreases at a constant rate",
    "Energy decreases at a rate proportional to its current value",
    "Energy changes at a rate equal to a constant minus a rate proportional to its current value",
    "Energy recovers at a rate which itself changes over time"
  ),
  Behaviour = c(
    "Static",
    "Linear decrease",
    "Exponential decrease towards zero",
    "Stability when recovery and depletion are equal",
    "Rise and collapse"
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kableExtra::kbl(eqn_interpretation_table,
  format = "markdown",
  caption = "Connecting Equations to Model Behaviour"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

```{r , fig.width=4, fig.height=2}
sfm <- stockflow()
print(sfm)
```

We update the simulation settings to model energy over the course of half a year (i.e., specified in days; note that the time unit merely changes the labels on the axes of the resulting plots, and does not affect the model's behaviour). Additionally, we set `only_stocks = FALSE` to return all variables in the simulation output, not just the stocks:

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, stop = round(365/2), time_units = "days",
 only_stocks = FALSE
 )
```

A model name can be supplied with `meta()`.

```{r}
sfm <- meta(sfm, name = "Burnout")
```

Next, we introduce a stock to the model to represent energy. Each variable requires a `name` such as `energy`, which serves as its identifier in equations. Each name should be unique and adhere to the same naming rules as R variables (e.g., no spaces or special characters). An optional `label` can be supplied for use in plots and diagrams (e.g., `label = "Energy Level"`); when omitted, the name is used.

Every stock also needs an *initial condition*: the value of the stock at the start of the simulation. This is set via the `eqn` argument, where here, we initialize energy at .3:

```{r}
sfm <- stock(sfm, name = energy, eqn = .3, label = "Energy")
```



```{r include=FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  sfm_list <- list()
  sfm_list[["A"]] <- sfm
}
```


Plotting the stock-and-flow model yields its stock-and-flow diagram, which now consists of only one stock:

```{r}
plot(sfm)
```

To assess its dynamics, we simulate the model over time and visualise the resulting timeseries:

```{r}
sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()
```

Across the entirety of the simulation, energy remains at its initial state. Stocks without flows are static, as there is no process specifying how they change. To deplete energy, we introduce an outflow representing energy depletion. For simplicity, we specify that depletion occurs at a constant rate over time, such as `.05`. Rather than defining the flow's `eqn` to be `.05` directly, we add a constant to the model, so that it can easily be changed later. This also helps to keep track of how parametrized the model is. 

```{r}
sfm <- constant(sfm, depletion_rate, eqn = .05, label = "Depletion Rate")
```

`eqn` is a generic argument used for all variable types, denoting the initial condition for a stock, a static value for a constant, and an equation that is recomputed at each time step in the simulation for flows and auxiliaries. `eqn` accepts any valid R expression that evaluates to a scalar, including functions (e.g., `sqrt()`, `min()`, `runif(1)`) and arithmetic operators (e.g., `*`, `+`). `eqn` can reference other variables defined in the model. 

`depletion_rate` can now be used as a variable in the equation for the outflow from energy:

```{r}
sfm <- flow(sfm, depletion,
  eqn = depletion_rate, from = energy,
  label = "Depletion"
)
```


```{r include=FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  sfm_list[["B"]] <- sfm
}
```



Aside from requiring a `name` and `eqn`, flows further need to be connected to a stock, at least as either an inflow (`to`) or an outflow (`from`).
Variable properties can be modified with `update()`. Note that by definition, outflows are subtracted from the stock, and as such do not need a minus sign in `eqn` to indicate that they decrease the stock. We simulate the model to check whether energy indeed depletes:

```{r}
sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()
```

As a stock with a constant outflow decreases linearly, energy becomes negative. To rectify this implausible behaviour, a naive solution may be to include a logical statement such as `ifelse(energy < 0, 0, energy)`. However, this computational trick would mask model misspecification. Ideally, stocks should remain within bounds due to plausible equations and parameters. For instance, we can prevent negative energy by making `depletion` proportional to the amount of available energy: `depletion_rate * energy`. In this way, when `energy` is zero, the outflow is also zero.

To assess whether this produces more plausible model behaviour, we modify the outflow using `update()`:


```{r}
sfm <- update(sfm, depletion, eqn = depletion_rate * energy)

sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()
```


```{r include=FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  sfm_list[["C"]] <- sfm
}
```


Energy now follows an exponential decay pattern, where depletion now depletes energy until it is zero, but not beyond this point. In other words, we have introduced a *feedback loop* to the system [@Meadows2008ThinkingPrimer]. Positive feedback loops amplify change, whereas negative feedback loops bring the system back to a target state [@Sterman2000BusinessWorld]. In our model, energy and depletion form a negative feedback loop that pulls energy to zero: the higher energy is, the more its outflow decreases it, until it reaches the implicit target state of zero.

To allow energy to recover, we introduce an inflow, again specified as a simple constant rate:

```{r}
sfm <- constant(sfm, recovery_rate, eqn = .3, label = "Recovery Rate") |>
  flow(recovery, eqn = recovery_rate, to = energy, label = "Recovery")

sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()
```



```{r include=FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  sfm_list[["D"]] <- sfm
}
```

We finally add an auxiliary to keep track of net flow to energy (inflow - outflow):

```{r}
sfm <- aux(sfm, net_flow, eqn = recovery - depletion, label = "Net flow to energy")

sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()

sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot(vars = c("depletion", "recovery", "net_flow"))
```



```{r include=FALSE, eval = has_internet(), error = TRUE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  
  ylim1 <- c(0, 6.1)
  ylim2 <- c(0, 0.35)
  vars <- c(
    "energy", 
    "depletion", "recovery",
    "net_flow"
  )
  colors <- c(stock_col, inflow_col, outflow_col, flow_col)

  x1 <- 0.5
  y1 <- 1.06
  y2 <- 0.48

  annot_ <- utils::modifyList(annot, list(
    font = list(size = font_size_subplot + 4, family = font_family)
  ))

  sim <- simulate(sfm, save_at = save_at_fine)

  pl1 <- plot(sim, vars = vars[1], xlab = "", main = "", line_width = 3, colors = colors[1]) |> rm_grid()

  colors <- COLORS[1 + seq_along(vars)]
  pl2 <- plot(sim, vars = vars[2:4], xlab = "", main = "", colors = colors[2:4]) |> rm_grid()

pl <- plotly::subplot(pl1, pl2,
    nrows = 2, shareX = TRUE, shareY = FALSE,
    titleY = FALSE, titleX = FALSE,
    margin = 0.05, heights = c(.5, .5)
  ) |>
    plotly::layout(
      font = annot_font,
      legend = utils::modifyList(annot_legend, list(
        y = -.09,
        traceorder = "normal",
        font = list(size = font_size_legend)
      )),
      annotations = list(
        utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(x = 0.5, y = -0.04))
      )
    ) |> 
    plotly::layout(
      yaxis = list(range = ylim1),
      yaxis2 = list(range = ylim2)
    ) |>
plotly::layout(annotations = list(
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = x1, y = y1, text = "Timeseries of Stock")),
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = x1, y = y2, text = "Timeseries of Flows and Auxiliary"))
      ))
 
  export_plot(pl, file.path(filepath_figs, "build_burnout_version1_sim.pdf"),
    width = 3.5, height = 4
  )
}
```



```{r include = FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {

  cat(plot(sfm, minlen = 1, nodesep = .15, pad = .1, 
  show_constants = TRUE, 
  order = c("net_flow", "energy"), align = c("net_flow", "energy"))$x$diagram)


{ 
 suffix <- '
 # Rank groupings
      {rank=same; "net_flow1"; "net_flow"; "invisnode1"; "energy"}

      "net_flow1" [id="net_flow1",label=<Net flow to energy<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = recovery - depletion</FONT>>,tooltip = "eqn = recovery - depletion",shape=plaintext, width=0.6, height=0.2, fillcolor="none", style=""]

      # Invisible edge to fix order
      "net_flow1" -> "net_flow" -> "invisnode1" -> "energy" [style=invis]

     # Define invisible node
      "invisnode1" [id="invisnode1",label="Invisible Node",shape=box,style=invis,fontsize=12,fontname="Times New Roman", width=.2, height=.1, fixedsize=true]
'
# Set xlabel = "" and label = "" of net_change

viz_str <- '
 digraph sfm {

      graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR, center=true, outputorder="edgesfirst", pad=0.1, nodesep=0.15, splines = true, concentrate = false]

      # Shared across all nodes (persists until overridden)
      node [fontsize=18,fontname="Times New Roman",fontcolor="black"]

      # Define stock nodes
      node [shape=box,style=filled,fillcolor="#83d3d4"]
      "energy" [id="energy",label=<Energy<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.3</FONT>>, tooltip = "Stock: Energy\nName: energy\nInitial value: 0.3\nInflows: Recovery\nOutflows: Depletion"]

      # Define flow nodes (intermediate nodes for flows)
      node [style = "",shape=plaintext, fontsize=16, width=0.6, height=0.3]
      "depletion" [id="depletion",label=<Depletion<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = depletion_rate *<BR/>energy</FONT>>, tooltip = "Flow: Depletion\nName: depletion\nEquation: depletion_rate * energy\nFrom: Energy\nTo: outside model boundary"]
        "recovery" [id="recovery",label=<Recovery<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = recovery_rate</FONT>>, tooltip = "Flow: Recovery\nName: recovery\nEquation: recovery_rate\nFrom: outside model boundary\nTo: Energy"]

      # Define external cloud nodes
      node [shape=doublecircle, fixedsize=true, width = .25, height = .25, orientation=15]
      "Cloud1" [label="", tooltip = "Outside model boundary\nSink of: Depletion"]
        "Cloud2" [label="", tooltip = "Outside model boundary\nSource of: Recovery"]

      # Define auxiliary nodes
      node [shape=circle,fontsize=16, width=0.15, height=0.15, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor="grey90"]
      "net_flow" [id="net_flow",xlabel="",label="", tooltip = "Auxiliary: Net flow to energy\nName: net_flow\nEquation: recovery - depletion"]

      # Define constant nodes
      node [shape=diamond,fontsize=16,width=0.15, height=0.15, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor="grey90"]
      "depletion_rate" [id="depletion_rate",xlabel=<<I>Depletion Rate</I><BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.05</FONT>>,label="", tooltip = "Constant: Depletion Rate\nName: depletion_rate\nValue: 0.05"]
        "recovery_rate" [id="recovery_rate",xlabel=<<I>Recovery Rate</I><BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.3</FONT>>,label="", tooltip = "Constant: Recovery Rate\nName: recovery_rate\nValue: 0.3"]

      # Define flow edges (stock -> flow_node)
      edge [style = "", arrowhead="none", color="black:#f48153:black", penwidth=1.1, minlen=1, tailport="e", headport="w"]
      "energy" -> "depletion"
        "Cloud2" -> "recovery"

      # Define flow edges (flow_node -> stock)
      edge [style = "", arrowhead="normal", color="black:#f48153:black", arrowsize=1.5, penwidth=1.1, minlen=1, tailport="e", headport="w"]
      "depletion" -> "Cloud1"
        "recovery" -> "energy"

      # Define dependency edges
      edge [style = "", color="#999999", arrowsize=0.8, penwidth=1, constraint=false, tailport = "_", headport="_"]
      "depletion_rate" -> "depletion"
        "energy" -> "depletion" [headport = "s", tailport = "s"]
        "recovery_rate" -> "recovery"
        "recovery" -> "net_flow"
        "depletion" -> "net_flow"

      # Ordering hints (invisible edges)
      edge [style=invis, constraint=true]
      "net_flow" -> "energy"


      # Rank groupings
            {rank=same; "depletion_rate"; "depletion" }
      {rank=same; "recovery_rate"; "recovery" }
      {rank=same; "net_flow"; "energy" }

    }
    '

}

# Find last closing brace and insert the suffix before it
viz_str <- sub("\\}$", paste0(suffix, "\n}"), viz_str)

  pl <- DiagrammeR::grViz(viz_str)
  pl

  export_plot(pl, file.path(filepath_figs, "build_burnout_version1_sfm.pdf"))
}
```

As a result of the new inflow, energy now stabilizes at a fixed level, as energy recovery and depletion balance out. 

Though the model no longer produces a negative energy state, our goal was to produce a burnout pattern. We thus need to revise the model.
What if the ability to recover is not static, but erodes over time? Put differently, what if the recovery rate is not a constant, but a stock? To implement this idea, we change the type of `recovery_rate`:

```{r}
sfm <- change_type(sfm, recovery_rate, new_type = stock)
```

We then add a new outflow that depletes the recovery rate in proportion to the amount worked:

```{r}
sfm <- flow(sfm, erosion,
  eqn = recovery_rate * depletion,
  from = recovery_rate, label = "Recovery Erosion"
)
sfm |>
  simulate() |>
  plot()
```


The plot shows how the erosion of the ability to recover produces a characteristic burnout pattern: a steep initial rise followed by a collapse of energy.

```{r include=FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  sfm_list[["E"]] <- sfm
}
```


```{r include=FALSE, eval = has_internet(), error = TRUE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  # Create subplot figure
  pl_list <- list()
  max_letter <- max(names(sfm_list))

  VARS_example <- c(
    "energy", 
    "depletion", "recovery",
    "net_flow"
  )

  # Recovery = recovery_rate so plot as one line
  sfm_list[["E"]] <- sfm_list[["E"]] |>
    update(recovery, label = "Recovery / recovery rate")

  COLORS_example <- COLORS[seq_along(VARS_example)]
  line_widths <- rep(line_width, length(VARS_example))
  line_widths[VARS_example == "energy"] <- line_width_stock

  titles <- paste0(
    paste0(
      "<i>", LETTERS[seq_along(sfm_list)], ". ",
      c(
        "Static Stock",
        "Constant Outflow",
        "Stock-Dependent<br>Outflow",
        "Constant Inflow",
        "Erosion of Recovery Rate"
        # "Auxiliary variable tracking net flow to energy"
      ), "</i>"
    )
  )
  titles <- stats::setNames(titles, names(sfm_list))


  annot_ <- utils::modifyList(annot, list(
    font = list(size = font_size_subplot + 4, family = font_family)
  ))


  for (letter in names(sfm_list)) {
    sim <- simulate(sfm_list[[letter]],
      save_at = save_at_fine, only_stocks = FALSE
    )

    vars_in_sim <- unique(sim$df$variable)

    vars_ <- VARS_example[VARS_example %in% vars_in_sim] 
    colors_ <- COLORS_example[VARS_example %in% vars_in_sim]
    colors_ <- adjustcolor(colors_, alpha.f = .9)
    line_widths_ <- line_widths[VARS_example %in% vars_in_sim]

    pl_list[[letter]] <- plot(sim,
      vars = vars_,
      colors = colors_,
      line_width = line_widths_,
      main = "",
      showlegend = (letter == max_letter)
    ) |>
      rm_grid() |>
      plotly::layout(yaxis = list(
        nticks = 3
      ), xaxis = list(
        tickvals = list(0, 7.5, 15)
      ))
  }

}
```




```{r include=FALSE, eval = has_internet(), error = TRUE}
if (recreate_figs) {
  # Version 2: all plots in one column

  y1 <- 1.01
  y2 <- 0.81
  y3 <- 0.63
  y4 <- 0.45
  y5 <- 0.275

  heights <- c(rep(.2, 4), .3)
  heights <- heights / sum(heights)

  titles <- paste0(
    paste0(
      "<i>", LETTERS[seq_along(sfm_list)], ". ",
      c(
        "Static Stock",
        "Constant Outflow",
        "Stock-Dependent Outflow",
        "Constant Inflow",
        "Erosion of Recovery Rate"
        # "Auxiliary variable tracking net flow to energy"
      ), "</i>"
    )
  )
  titles <- stats::setNames(titles, names(sfm_list))

  pl <- plotly::subplot(pl_list,
    nrows = length(pl_list),
    shareX = TRUE, shareY = FALSE,
    titleY = FALSE, titleX = FALSE,
    margin = 0.0275,
     heights = heights
  ) |>
    plotly::layout(
    yaxis = list(range = c(0.1, 0.5)),
    # yaxis2 = list(showline = TRUE),
    yaxis3 = list(range = c(0, 0.35)),
    # yaxis4 = list(showline = TRUE),
    # yaxis5 = list(showline = TRUE),

      font = annot_font,
      legend = utils::modifyList(annot_legend, list(
        y = -.09,
        traceorder = "normal",
        font = list(size = font_size_legend)
      )),
      annotations = list(
        utils::modifyList(annot_xlab, list(x = 0.5, y = -0.04))
      )
    ) |>
    plotly::layout(annotations = list(
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = 0.5, y = y1, text = titles[["A"]])),
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = 0.5, y = y2, text = titles[["B"]])),
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = 0.5, y = y3, text = titles[["C"]])),
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = 0.5, y = y4, text = titles[["D"]])),
      utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = 0.5, y = y5, text = titles[["E"]]))
      # utils::modifyList(annot_, list(x = .5, y = -.5, text = "F. Stock-and-Flow Diagram"))
    ))

  export_plot(pl, file.path(filepath_figs, "build_sim_column.pdf"),
    width = 2.5, height = 8
  )
}
```


```{r include = FALSE}
if (recreate_figs) {

  cat(plot(sfm, minlen = 1, nodesep = .15, pad = .1, 
  show_constants = TRUE,
  order = c("net_flow", "energy", "recovery_rate"), align = c("net_flow", "energy", "recovery_rate"))$x$diagram)

  {

    suffix <- '
    ### Additions
 # Rank groupings
      {rank=same; "net_flow1"; "net_flow"; "invisnode1"; "energy"; "invisnode2"; "recovery_rate"}


      # Invisible edge to fix order
      "net_flow1" -> "net_flow" -> "invisnode1" -> "energy" -> "invisnode2" -> "recovery_rate" [style=invis]

     # Define invisible node
      "invisnode1" [id="invisnode1",label="Invisible Node",shape=box,style=invis,fontsize=12,fontname="Times New Roman", width=.2, height=.1, fixedsize=true]
      "invisnode2" [id="invisnode2",label="Invisible Node",shape=box,style=invis,fontsize=12,fontname="Times New Roman", width=.2, height=.1, fixedsize=true]
'

# Set xlabel = "" and label = "" of net_change

    viz_str <- '

     digraph sfm {

      graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR, center=true, outputorder="edgesfirst", pad=0.1, nodesep=0.15, splines = true, concentrate = false]

      # Shared across all nodes (persists until overridden)
      node [fontsize=18,fontname="Times New Roman",fontcolor="black"]

      # Define stock nodes
      node [shape=box,style=filled,fillcolor="#83d3d4"]
      "energy" [id="energy",label=<Energy<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.3</FONT>>, tooltip = "Stock: Energy\nName: energy\nInitial value: 0.3\nInflows: Recovery\nOutflows: Depletion"]
        "recovery_rate" [id="recovery_rate",label=<Recovery Rate<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.3</FONT>>, tooltip = "Stock: Recovery Rate\nName: recovery_rate\nInitial value: 0.3\nInflows: —\nOutflows: Recovery Erosion"]

      # Define flow nodes (intermediate nodes for flows)
      node [style = "",shape=plaintext, fontsize=16, width=0.6, height=0.3]
      "depletion" [id="depletion",label=<Depletion<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = depletion_rate *<BR/>energy</FONT>>, tooltip = "Flow: Depletion\nName: depletion\nEquation: depletion_rate * energy\nFrom: Energy\nTo: outside model boundary"]
        "erosion" [id="erosion",label=<Recovery Erosion<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = recovery_rate *<BR/>depletion</FONT>>, tooltip = "Flow: Recovery Erosion\nName: erosion\nEquation: recovery_rate * depletion\nFrom: Recovery Rate\nTo: outside model boundary"]
        "recovery" [id="recovery",label=<Recovery<BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = recovery_rate</FONT>>, tooltip = "Flow: Recovery\nName: recovery\nEquation: recovery_rate\nFrom: outside model boundary\nTo: Energy"]

      # Define external cloud nodes
      node [shape=doublecircle, fixedsize=true, width = .25, height = .25, orientation=15]
      "Cloud1" [label="", tooltip = "Outside model boundary\nSink of: Depletion"]
        "Cloud2" [label="", tooltip = "Outside model boundary\nSink of: Recovery Erosion"]
        "Cloud3" [label="", tooltip = "Outside model boundary\nSource of: Recovery"]

      # Define auxiliary nodes
      node [shape=circle,fontsize=16, width=0.15, height=0.15, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor="grey90"]
      "net_flow" [id="net_flow",xlabel="",label="", tooltip = "Auxiliary: Net flow to energy\nName: net_flow\nEquation: recovery - depletion"]

      # Define constant nodes
      node [shape=diamond,fontsize=16,width=0.15, height=0.15, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor="grey90"]
      "depletion_rate" [id="depletion_rate",xlabel=<<I>Depletion Rate</I><BR/><FONT POINT-SIZE="13" COLOR="black">eqn = 0.05</FONT>>,label="", tooltip = "Constant: Depletion Rate\nName: depletion_rate\nValue: 0.05"]

      # Define flow edges (stock -> flow_node)
      edge [style = "", arrowhead="none", color="black:#f48153:black", penwidth=1.1, minlen=1, tailport="e", headport="w"]
      "energy" -> "depletion"
        "recovery_rate" -> "erosion"
        "Cloud3" -> "recovery"

      # Define flow edges (flow_node -> stock)
      edge [style = "", arrowhead="normal", color="black:#f48153:black", arrowsize=1.5, penwidth=1.1, minlen=1, tailport="e", headport="w"]
      "depletion" -> "Cloud1"
        "erosion" -> "Cloud2"
        "recovery" -> "energy"

      # Define dependency edges
      edge [style = "", color="#999999", arrowsize=0.8, penwidth=1, constraint=false, tailport = "_", headport="_"]
      "depletion_rate" -> "depletion"
        "energy" -> "depletion" [headport = "s", tailport = "s"]
        "recovery_rate" -> "erosion" [headport = "n", tailport = "n"]
        "depletion" -> "erosion" [headport = "s", tailport = "s"]
        "recovery_rate" -> "recovery" [headport = "n", tailport = "n"]
        "recovery" -> "net_flow"
        "depletion" -> "net_flow"

      # Ordering hints (invisible edges)
      edge [style=invis, constraint=true]
      "net_flow" -> "energy"
      "energy" -> "recovery_rate"


      # Rank groupings
            {rank=same; "depletion_rate"; "depletion" }
      {rank=same; "net_flow"; "energy"; "recovery_rate" }

    }
    '
  }

  viz_str <- sub("\\}$", paste0(suffix, "\n}"), viz_str)

  pl <- DiagrammeR::grViz(viz_str)
  pl

  export_plot(pl, file.path(filepath_figs, "build_burnout_version2_sfm.pdf"))
}
```

The net flow to energy is initially positive, as recovery exceeds energy depletion from depletion. As erosion progressively reduces the recovery rate, this inflow weakens, eventually falling below the outflow from depletion. The net flow to energy is negative, and energy begins to decline, leading to the observed collapse. This complex behaviour is produced by a simple stock-and-flow model consisting of two stocks and three flows:

```{r}
plot(sfm)
```



Note that this is identical to the version stored in the model library, which can be loaded using `stockflow()`:

```{r}
sfm <- stockflow("burnout", version = 2)
```


In summary, stock-and-flow models consist of one or more stocks, each requiring an inflow and/or outflow to change over time. Without limiting processes, stocks may continue to increase indefinitely; without restorative processes, stocks may deplete past the point of recovery. In principle, inflows and outflows connected to the same stock could be combined into a single net flow (as often done in differential equation models). However, separating inflows and outflows encourages more precise thinking about what processes increase and decrease stocks, and what distinct information and rates of change govern each flow [@Sterman2000BusinessWorld, p. 547]. Disaggregation further reframes interventions by for example identifying whether to limit inflows or promote outflows [@Levine1993SystemProblems; @Meadows2008ThinkingPrimer].

## Variable types

```{r echo = FALSE}
variable_types_table <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = c(
    "Role in system",
    "Varies within time horizon",
    "A process taking place over time",
    "Can be captured at any given moment in time",
    "`eqn` denotes",
    "Allowed dependencies in `eqn`",
    "Examples"
  ),
  Stock = c(
    "Defines the state of the system; accumulates the effects of flow(s) over time",
    "✓",
    "✗",
    "✓",
    "Initial condition",
    "Constants and (initial values of) other stocks",
    "Emotions, beliefs, stress, trust, resources"
  ),
  Flow = c(
    "Increases or decreases a stock",
    "✓",
    "✓",
    "✗",
    "Flow rate computed at every time step",
    "Any other variable",
    "Coping, learning, emotion regulation"
  ),
  Constant = c(
    "Specifies static quantity",
    "✗",
    "✗",
    "✓",
    "Fixed value",
    "Other constants and (initial values of) stocks",
    "Rates, capacities, thresholds"
  ),
  Auxiliary = c(
    "Provides intermediate computations for convenience; keeps track of changing quantities",
    "✓",
    "Possibly",
    "Possibly",
    "Value computed at every time step",
    "Any other variable",
    "Performance indices, ratios, sums of stocks"
  )
)

kableExtra::kbl(variable_types_table,
  format = "markdown",
  col.names = c("Characteristic", "Stock", "Flow", "Constant", "Auxiliary"),
  caption = "Characteristics of Variable Types in Stock-and-Flow Models"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

The following flowchart can be used to determine a variable's type:

```{r include = FALSE}
{
  viz_str <- "
digraph flowchart {

  graph [layout = dot,
         rankdir = TB,
         bgcolor = white,
         nodesep = 0.45,
         ranksep = 0.5,
         splines = ortho,
         fontname = '%(font_family)s',
         pad = '0.2']

  node [fontsize = 9.5,
        fontname = '%(font_family)s',
        fontcolor = 'white',
        penwidth = 0]

  edge [fontname = '%(font_family)s',
        fontsize = 8.5,
        fontcolor = '#555555',
        color = '#666666',
        penwidth = 1.3,
        arrowsize = 0.6]

  # --- DECISION NODES (rounded boxes with border) ---
  q1 [label = 'Can the variable be represented\\nas a continuous quantity that\\nbe lower or higher?',
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.15,0.1']

  q2 [label = 'Does the variable change within\\nthe chosen time horizon?',
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.15,0.1']

  q3 [label = 'Is the variable a behaviour, action,\\nor process that takes place over time?',
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.15,0.1']

  q4 [label = 'Does the variable accumulate\\nthe effects of flows?',
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.15,0.1']

  q5 [label = 'Does the variable\\'s effect\\naccumulate in a stock?', # Does it directly increase or decrease a stock?
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.15,0.1']

  q6 [label = 'Should it?',
      shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
      fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
      color = '#0f5c5a',
      penwidth = 2, margin = '0.12,0.08']

  # --- TERMINAL NODES (no border, darker fill) ---
  constant [label = 'Constant',
            shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
            fillcolor = '#0f5c5a', color = 'none',
            fontsize = 10, margin = '0.15,0.08']

  stock [label = 'Stock',
         shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
         fillcolor = '#0f5c5a', color = 'none',
         fontsize = 10, margin = '0.15,0.08']

  flow [label = 'Flow',
        shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
        fillcolor = '#0f5c5a', color = 'none',
        fontsize = 10, margin = '0.15,0.08']

  auxiliary [label = 'Auxiliary',
             shape = box, style = 'filled,rounded',
             fillcolor = '#0f5c5a', color = 'none',
             fontsize = 10, margin = '0.15,0.08']

  # --- ACTION NODES ---
  reformulate [label = 'Reformulate variable',
               shape = box, style = 'dashed,rounded,filled',
            fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
            fontsize = 8, penwidth = 1.2, color = '#17807c',
            margin = '0.1,0.06']

  add_flow [label = 'Add missing\\nflows',
            shape = box, style = 'dashed,rounded,filled',
            fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
            fontsize = 8, penwidth = 1.2, color = '#17807c',
            margin = '0.1,0.06']

  add_stock [label = 'Add missing\\nstock',
             shape = box, style = 'dashed,rounded,filled',
             fillcolor = '#e8f4f3', fontcolor = '#0f5c5a',
             fontsize = 8, penwidth = 1.2, color = '#17807c',
             margin = '0.1,0.06']

  # --- EDGES ---
  q1 -> reformulate [xlabel = 'No', minlen = 2]
  reformulate -> q1 [minlen = 2, style = dashed, penwidth = 1]
  q1 -> q2          [xlabel = 'Yes']

  q2 -> constant [xlabel = 'No', minlen = 2]
  q2 -> q3       [xlabel = 'Yes']

  q3 -> q4 [xlabel = <<font>No, it can be captured<br/>at a single moment in time</font>>]
  q3 -> q5 [xlabel = '  Yes']

  q4 -> stock [xlabel = 'Yes']
  q4 -> q6    [xlabel = 'No']

  q5 -> flow  [xlabel = 'Yes']
  q5 -> q6    [xlabel = 'No']

  q6 -> auxiliary [xlabel = 'No']
  q6 -> add_flow  [xlabel = 'Yes', style = dashed, color = '#2a9d8f', penwidth = 1]
  q6 -> add_stock [xlabel = 'Yes', style = dashed, color = '#2a9d8f', penwidth = 1]
  add_flow  -> stock [style = dashed, color = '#2a9d8f', penwidth = 1]
  add_stock -> flow  [style = dashed, color = '#2a9d8f', penwidth = 1]

  # --- RANK CONSTRAINTS ---
  { rank = same; q1; reformulate }
  { rank = same; q2; constant }
  { rank = same; q4; q5 }
  { rank = same; stock; q6; flow }
  { rank = same; add_flow; auxiliary; add_stock }
}
"
}

viz_str <- sprintf_arg(
  viz_str,
  list(
    font_family = font_family
  )
)

pl <- DiagrammeR::grViz(viz_str)
pl
```



## Overview of package functionality


```{r echo=FALSE}
functions_table <- data.frame(
  Function = c("`stockflow()`", "`stock()`", "`flow()`", "`constant()`", "`aux()`", "`lookup()`", "`update()`", "`simulate()`", "`plot()`", "`summary()`", "`as.data.frame()`", "`sim_settings()`", "`meta()`", "`export_model()`"),
  Purpose = c("Create empty model or load template", "Add or modify a stock", "Add or modify a flow", "Add or modify a constant", "Add or modify an auxiliary", "Add or modify a lookup function", "Add or modify any variable (generic)", "Simulate model", "Plot model diagram or simulation", "Run model diagnostics", "Get model properties in a dataframe", "Modify simulation specifications", "Modify model metadata", "Export models to other formats")
)

kableExtra::kbl(functions_table,
  format = "markdown",
  col.names = c("Function", "Purpose"),
  caption = "Main functions in *sdbuildR*"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```


In some cases, it may be useful to refer to global simulation variables in the model's equations:

```{r echo=FALSE}
functions_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = c(
    "`times`", "`t`", "`dt`"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Vector with simulation times",
    "Current time in the ODE",
    "Time step of the simulation"
  ),
  Use.case = c(
    "Use in equations, e.g., `pulse(times, 5, width = dt)`",
    "Use in equations, e.g., `input(t)`",
    "Use in equations, e.g., `pulse(times, 5, width = dt)`"
  )
)

kableExtra::kbl(functions_table,
  format = "markdown",
  col.names = c(
    "Variable", "Description", "Use case"
  ),
  caption = "Global simulation variables in *sdbuildR*"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```


### Simulation specifications

We may want to observe the system over a longer time period, or with a different time step.

```{r}
sfm <- sfm |>
  sim_settings(
    start = 0,
    stop = 250,
    dt = 0.001
  )
```


Simulation settings can be set directly on the model object as above, or passed to `simulate()`:

```{r}
sim <- simulate(sfm, start = 0, stop = 250)
```

`dt` refers to the time step of the simulation, which determines how often the model's equations are evaluated. A smaller `dt` can increase the accuracy of the simulation, but also increases computational time and the size of the resulting dataframe. To reduce the saved output, we may save fewer timepoints, for instance, every 0.1 days:

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, save_at = 0.1)
```

Or specific time points:
```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, save_at = c(1, 50, 100))
```

Alternatively, we can specify the number of time points to save with `save_n`:

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, save_n = 100)
```

Similarly, we may change the numerical method used to solve the model. The default method is `"euler"`, which is the simplest numerical integration method. For more complex models or when higher accuracy is needed, consider other methods like `"rk4"`:

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, method = "rk4")
```

All available simulation methods can found with:

```{r}
sim_methods()
```

Note that some methods may not be available in Julia and vice versa.

In case the simulation contains stochastic elements, we may want to set a seed to ensure that the simulation is reproducible. For example, the initial value of energy could be a random number:

```{r}
sfm <- stock(sfm, energy, eqn = runif(1, 0, 1))
```

The seed needs to be an integer:

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, seed = 123)
```

The seed can also be removed to ensure variation in the simulation. This can be useful to for example test the sensitivity of the model to initial condition variation. 

```{r}
sfm <- sim_settings(sfm, seed = NULL)
```


### Renaming variables

Variable names can easily be changed:

```{r}
sfm <- change_name(sfm, recovery_rate, new_name = recovery_store)
```

This will ensure that all references to `recovery_rate` are changed to `recovery_store`. 

### Allowed variable names

When creating variables or changing variable names, a warning may be issued that the name was modified to be syntactically valid and unique. For example:

```{r error = TRUE, warning = TRUE}
sfm <- change_name(sfm, recovery_rate, new_name = t)
```

The name `t` is not usable, as this already refers to the current time step. Similarly, names cannot contain spaces or special characters:

```{r error = TRUE}
sfm <- change_name(sfm, recovery_rate, new_name = recovery - rate)
```

Names also cannot be duplicated:

```{r error = TRUE, warning = TRUE}
sfm <- change_name(sfm, energy, new_name = recovery)
```

### Removing variables

To remove a variable from the model, use `discard()`:

```{r}
sfm <- discard(sfm, net_flow)
```

Note that this cannot be undone!

### Lookup functions

Lookup functions (`lookup`), also known as table or graphical functions, are interpolation functions used to create custom input-output functions, where we define the desired output (y) for a specified input (x). They are defined by a set of x- and y-domain points. The interpolation method defines the behaviour of the lookup function between x-points, and the extrapolation method defines the behaviour outside of the x-points. For example, a simple lookup function called `"graph"` may look like this:

```{r}
sfm <- stockflow() |>
  lookup(graph,
    xpts = c(0, 1, 2), ypts = c(0.5, 1, 1),
    interpolation = "linear", extrapolation = "nearest"
  )
```

The function can now be used in any equation in the model like so:

```{r}
sfm <- constant(sfm, x, eqn = graph(1))
```


### Custom functions 

New functions can be defined such that they can be used anywhere in the model. For example, if the `logistic()` function did not exist, you could create it yourself:

```{r}
sfm <- stockflow() |>
  custom_func(f, eqn = function(x, slope = 1, midpoint = .5) 1 / (1 + exp(-slope * (x - midpoint))))
```

This will create a function `f()` that can be used in any equation in the model like so: 

```{r}
sfm <- constant(sfm, x, eqn = f(0))
```




### Documenting

To document meta-properties of the model, use `meta()`. For example, the model's `name`, subtitle (`caption`), or `author`. `meta()` accepts any key-value pair, so custom metadata can also be added.

```{r}
sfm <- meta(sfm, author = "Kyra Evers", affiliation = "University of Amsterdam")
```
